content.text: What it is

A CoE Service Catalog is a curated, formally documented portfolio of services that the AI Center of Excellence offers to agent teams and other internal consumers. It describes each service's capabilities, service-level agreements, pricing model, intake process, and support channels in a standardized format. For agentic AI programs specifically, the catalog bridges the gap between the CoE's shared infrastructure (LLM gateways, vector stores, evaluation harnesses) and the development teams that need to consume those capabilities to build autonomous agents without reinventing foundational components.
Why it matters

Without a service catalog, agent teams spend significant time either rebuilding infrastructure that already exists centrally or hunting for it through informal channels. The catalog eliminates this waste and creates network effects: each team that uses the CoE's LLM gateway concentrates usage for better rate negotiation; each team that contributes patterns to the shared library reduces bootstrap time for the next team. From a governance perspective, the catalog makes the CoE's value proposition concrete and measurable — service utilization, SLA compliance, and cost savings become reportable metrics that justify the CoE's operating budget at quarterly steering reviews.
Benefits
Reduces time-to-first-agent for new teams by providing pre-built, pre-secured infrastructure they can consume via API without platform setup.
Centralizes LLM API key management and rate limiting, preventing teams from independently holding credentials that create security exposure.
Creates a single source of truth for approved AI capabilities, so teams don't independently evaluate and contract with vendors the CoE has already assessed.
Enables the CoE to measure adoption, identify underutilized services, and retire or consolidate offerings that don't deliver value.
Provides a mechanism for the CoE to enforce responsible AI requirements at the infrastructure layer rather than relying on teams to self-certify.
Trade-offs and limitations
A catalog that doesn't meet SLAs is worse than no catalog — teams build workarounds and distrust the CoE, which is harder to recover from than the initial absence of centralized services.
Designing services that fit diverse consumer needs (research teams, production engineers, business analysts) requires abstraction that may not serve any consumer optimally.
Maintaining a growing catalog demands engineering resources that may crowd out the CoE's ability to do forward-looking work on emerging AI capabilities.
Chargeback models for catalog services create friction in early adoption phases, particularly when teams have not yet proven ROI for their AI projects.
How it works

The service catalog organizes offerings into tiers based on consumption model. Foundation services are always-on infrastructure: the LLM gateway (rate-limited, logged, cost-allocated API proxy to model providers), the enterprise vector store (managed Pinecone or Weaviate with data governance controls), the agent runtime (containerized execution environment for autonomous agents), and the observability stack (traces, metrics, and prompt logs for debugging and compliance). These services have the most demanding SLAs because everything else depends on them.

Platform services sit above foundation and provide higher-level capabilities: managed MLOps pipelines for fine-tuning and deploying custom models, an evaluation harness for systematic agent benchmarking, and a prompt registry that stores versioned prompt templates with performance metadata. These services have more complex intake processes because they require provisioning dedicated resources per consumer team.

Enablement services are time-bounded: agent starter kits (scaffolded project templates with security defaults baked in), training workshops (quarterly multi-day immersives plus self-paced modules), a pattern library of reusable agentic patterns (tool-use, memory architectures, multi-agent orchestration), and office hours (weekly 30-minute slots with CoE engineers). Advisory services are on-demand: use-case triage sessions (structured 2-hour reviews with the CoE team), architecture review (formal pre-production readiness check), responsible AI assessment (bias and risk evaluation), and vendor guidance (evaluation support for new AI vendor selection).
Architecture

attrs.alt: CoE service catalog layered architecture showing foundation, platform, enablement, and advisory service tiers
attrs.title: CoE Service Catalog for Agent Teams

content.text: The four-layer architecture reflects increasing abstraction and decreasing service frequency. Foundation services run continuously and are consumed programmatically via API. Platform services are provisioned per-project and managed through a deployment pipeline. Enablement services are time-bounded engagements consumed by people. Advisory services are event-driven consultations. Each consumer team interacts with the catalog through a self-service portal that shows available services, SLA status, and pricing, plus an intake form to request provisioning or advisory sessions.
When to use it

A formalized service catalog is warranted when the CoE has at least 3 distinct consumer teams and is running more than 2 services. Below this threshold, informal coordination is sufficient and catalog overhead is not justified. The catalog becomes essential when: multiple teams are independently building similar infrastructure, when teams report confusion about what the CoE offers vs. what they must build, or when the CoE's quarterly steering report shows rising costs with unclear attribution of value.
How to implement
Audit existing CoE capabilities and informal services currently consumed by teams — this is the raw input for the initial catalog.
Interview 3–5 active consumer teams to identify unmet needs and pain points with current informal service delivery.

Define the service template: for each offering, document the service name, description, tier (foundation/platform/enablement/advisory), SLA targets, pricing model, intake process, support channel, and deprecation policy.

Start with 3–5 high-demand services in v1. Resist the temptation to catalog everything at once — a large catalog with inconsistent SLAs degrades trust faster than a small catalog with reliable delivery.
Build the intake workflow: a self-service portal or at minimum a standardized intake form (Jira template or ServiceNow catalog item) so requests are trackable and SLA clock starts automatically.
Instrument all foundation services with usage telemetry from day one — you need adoption data to justify the catalog's value to the steering committee.
Publish the catalog on the corporate intranet with a clear owner, update cadence, and feedback mechanism. A catalog that teams cannot find is a catalog that doesn't exist.
Examples

# Service catalog entry: LLM Gateway
service_id: coe-llm-gateway-v2
name: Enterprise LLM Gateway
tier: foundation
description: |
  Rate-limited, cost-allocated, logged API proxy to approved LLM providers
  (OpenAI GPT-4o, Anthropic Claude Sonnet, Mistral Large). Handles auth,
  retry logic, circuit-breaking, and per-team cost allocation automatically.
consumers:
  - agent-teams
  - data-science
  - product-engineering
interface: REST API, Python SDK (coe-llm-client v1.x)
sla:
  availability: 99.5%
  p95_latency_ms: 2000  # gateway overhead only, excludes provider latency
  incident_response: 30min
  support_hours: 24x7
pricing:
  model: pass-through
  description: Actual LLM API cost + 8% CoE overhead (covers infra + support)
  billing_granularity: per-1k-tokens by team
quotas:
  default_rpm: 500  # requests per minute per team
  burst_rpm: 2000   # for 60-second bursts
intake:
  self_service: true
  process: Submit API key request at coe-portal.internal/llm-gateway
  provisioning_sla: 1 business day
support:
  channel: '#coe-llm-gateway' on Slack
  escalation: coe-oncall@company.com
owners:
  primary: AI Platform Team
  backup: CoE Director
last_reviewed: 2025-06-01
next_review: 2025-12-01
status: GA

# Agent team consuming CoE LLM Gateway via the SDK
from coe_llm_client import CoELLMClient
import os

# Auth via service account token issued at intake
client = CoELLMClient(
    token=os.environ["COE_GATEWAY_TOKEN"],
    team_id="retail-pricing-agent",
    base_url="https://llm-gateway.coe.internal/v2"
)

# Identical interface to provider SDK — gateway handles routing, retry, cost tracking
response = client.chat(
    model="claude-sonnet",  # maps to approved model version internally
    messages=[
        {"role": "system", "content": "You are a pricing analyst agent."},
        {"role": "user", "content": "Analyze the attached pricing data and recommend adjustments."}
    ],
    max_tokens=2048,
    temperature=0.1
)

print(response.content)
# Usage is automatically attributed to 'retail-pricing-agent' team
# CoE dashboard shows cost, latency, and token consumption per team

# Service catalog query API — teams can discover available services programmatically
import requests

catalog = requests.get(
    "https://coe-portal.internal/api/catalog",
    headers={"Authorization": "Bearer " + os.environ["COE_PORTAL_TOKEN"]},
    params={"tier": "foundation", "status": "GA", "consumer": "agent-teams"}
).json()

for service in catalog["services"]:
    print(f"{service['service_id']}: {service['name']}")
    print(f"  SLA availability: {service['sla']['availability']}")
    print(f"  Intake: {service['intake']['process']}")
    print()

# Output:
# coe-llm-gateway-v2: Enterprise LLM Gateway
#   SLA availability: 99.5%
#   Intake: Submit API key request at coe-portal.internal/llm-gateway
#
# coe-vector-store-v1: Enterprise Vector Store
#   SLA availability: 99.9%
#   Intake: Provision namespace via coe-portal.internal/vector-store
Real-world example

A global insurance company's AI CoE launched its service catalog after identifying that five agent teams were independently maintaining their own LLM API integrations, each with distinct authentication patterns, retry logic, and logging implementations. The total engineering overhead across five teams was estimated at 0.4 FTE-equivalent annually in maintenance effort.

The CoE built the LLM Gateway as a foundation service and migrated all five teams to it within 90 days. Each team saved an average of 12 engineering hours per month in API maintenance and debugging. More importantly, the centralized gateway gave the CoE visibility into total LLM spend for the first time — and the concentration of usage enabled them to negotiate an enterprise agreement with their primary model provider that reduced per-token costs by 22%.

The catalog's evaluation harness became the second-most-used service. Agent teams used it to run systematic regression tests before deploying new prompt versions or model upgrades, catching three regressions in the first quarter that would have reached production under the previous informal testing approach. The CoE reported both the prevented incidents and the cost savings in the quarterly steering committee review, securing funding for two additional platform engineers.
Common mistakes
Launching with too many services before any have proven SLA reliability — a catalog with 15 services of inconsistent quality is worse than 5 reliable ones.
Publishing a catalog but not instrumenting usage, leaving the CoE unable to measure adoption or justify the investment.
Setting SLA availability targets (e.g., 99.9%) without having the on-call rotation and incident response process to back them up.
Treating the catalog as a static document rather than a living product — services need quarterly reviews, deprecation cycles, and versioning.
Ignoring the agent runtime service — teams building autonomous agents need more than an LLM API; they need managed execution environments with tool sandboxing, memory management, and audit logging.
Best practices
Use a standard service template for every catalog entry so consumers can compare services on consistent dimensions without reading unstructured prose.
Publish service health status dashboards that are accessible to all consumer teams without requiring a support ticket — most outage complaints come from lack of visibility, not the outage itself.
Version catalog services semantically (v1, v2) and maintain N-1 support for at least 6 months before deprecating older versions.
Assign explicit service owners who are accountable for SLA compliance, not just teams. A service owned by a team is often owned by no one.
Run quarterly catalog reviews: retire services with fewer than 3 active consumers, promote high-adoption alpha services to GA, and roadmap the next 2 services based on unmet demand.
For agent-specific services, include a sandbox tier that allows teams to experiment at zero cost, reducing friction for new agent team onboarding.
Performance and scalability

Foundation services must be architected for the aggregate load of all consumer teams simultaneously. The LLM Gateway is typically the highest-load service and should be deployed with horizontal scaling, regional redundancy, and circuit-breaker patterns that prevent one team's traffic spike from degrading service for others. Use token-bucket rate limiting per team rather than global rate limits, which prevents any single consumer from starving others during peak usage.

As the catalog scales to 10+ consumer teams, the intake and provisioning workflow becomes the bottleneck. Automate provisioning for foundation services: team onboarding should be self-service and complete in under 5 minutes. Reserve human-in-the-loop provisioning only for platform services that require dedicated resource allocation and for advisory services where CoE judgment is inherently required.

Security and reliability

Each catalog service entry must specify its data classification ceiling — the most sensitive data class it may process. This prevents teams from routing confidential or restricted data through foundation services that were only tested and approved for internal-class data. The LLM Gateway should enforce data classification restrictions programmatically: requests that include tokens indicating restricted data scope should be rejected or routed to an isolated endpoint with additional audit controls.

For reliability, maintain a dedicated non-production environment for each catalog service so teams can test against realistic infrastructure without risking production SLA impacts. Foundation services should have documented runbooks for their most common failure modes, and those runbooks should be part of the catalog entry so consumer teams can self-triage before escalating to the CoE on-call engineer.
Key takeaways
A service catalog makes the CoE's value proposition concrete and measurable, converting informal knowledge into trackable service delivery.
Start with 3–5 high-demand, reliable services rather than a comprehensive catalog of inconsistent quality — reliability wins trust, breadth alone does not.
Instrument every foundation service from launch so adoption data is available at the first quarterly steering review.
Use a tiered service model: foundation (always-on), platform (per-project), enablement (time-bounded), advisory (event-driven) to match SLA requirements to cost of delivery.
For agent teams specifically, prioritize the LLM gateway and agent runtime — these two services eliminate the most common infrastructure reinvention patterns across teams.
Automate provisioning for foundation services; reserve human-in-the-loop only for platform and advisory services where CoE judgment is required.
Treat the catalog as a product with quarterly review cycles, version management, and explicit deprecation policies — static catalogs decay into irrelevance.
